title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear an instructor called Jack talking about a youth kayaking programme. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear an instructor called Jack talking about a youth kayaking programme. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A club instructor called Jack describes a youth kayaking programme.
extracts.audio_script: Hello everybody. I’m Jack, an instructor at Blue River Kayak Club, and I’m going to explain our programme for young people this summer. Despite the club’s name, our first lessons are not held on the river, where the current may be fast after rain. Beginners start on a sheltered [9]canal[/9] beside the town park. It is wide enough to practise safely and easy for instructors to supervise from the bank. Only after learners have developed control do we arrange river trips in small groups.||The programme is suitable for twelve- to seventeen-year-olds, including people who have never been in a kayak before. Some students think the first lesson is mainly about paddling quickly. Actually, before anyone enters the water, the instructors teach how to adjust and wear a [10]life jacket[/10] correctly. All boats and safety equipment are provided. Participants need clothing which can get wet, a change of clothes and a pair of [11]old trainers[/11]; sandals are not suitable because they may come off in the water. There are changing rooms available beside the canal.||During early sessions, students work in pairs and practise simple turns and stopping. We sometimes use games to make this enjoyable, but safety always comes first. The first award participants can work towards is based not on speed, but on [12]boat control[/12], showing they can follow directions calmly and safely. More experienced members may later join a trip to a small [13]island[/13] further along the river, where the group stops for lunch before paddling back. This day trip is only offered when weather conditions are suitable and instructors are satisfied with participants' ability.||We take care of the environment around us. Sadly, rubbish occasionally floats near the waterways, especially after busy weekends in the park. Once each month, club members assist with a [14]river clean-up[/14], collecting safe items from the banks while adults remove anything dangerous. Last summer, some members produced posters about litter. This year they have decided to create a short [15]safety video[/15] showing new learners what to expect at their first session. It will be placed on the club website and shown at our welcome meeting. Parents will need to give permission for anyone appearing in it.||Students who attend all six beginner sessions are presented with a [16]club badge[/16] at the end, and they may then apply for weekend activities. Our guest session this summer will be led by an athlete who specialises in [17]kayak racing[/17]; she will talk about training and demonstrate some advanced techniques, although beginners will not be expected to copy them. Lastly, we need to replace several ageing paddles. The club will raise money through a [18]sponsored paddle[/18] in August, with members completing a safe distance on the canal. Information about registration and medical forms is on the table by the door.

dialogue.speaker: jack
dialogue.text: Hello everybody. I’m Jack, an instructor at Blue River Kayak Club, and I’m going to explain our programme for young people this summer. Despite the club’s name, our first lessons are not held on the river, where the current may be fast after rain. Beginners start on a sheltered canal beside the town park. It is wide enough to practise safely and easy for instructors to supervise from the bank. Only after learners have developed control do we arrange river trips in small groups.||The programme is suitable for twelve- to seventeen-year-olds, including people who have never been in a kayak before. Some students think the first lesson is mainly about paddling quickly. Actually, before anyone enters the water, the instructors teach how to adjust and wear a life jacket correctly. All boats and safety equipment are provided. Participants need clothing which can get wet, a change of clothes and a pair of old trainers; sandals are not suitable because they may come off in the water. There are changing rooms available beside the canal.||During early sessions, students work in pairs and practise simple turns and stopping. We sometimes use games to make this enjoyable, but safety always comes first. The first award participants can work towards is based not on speed, but on boat control, showing they can follow directions calmly and safely. More experienced members may later join a trip to a small island further along the river, where the group stops for lunch before paddling back. This day trip is only offered when weather conditions are suitable and instructors are satisfied with participants' ability.||We take care of the environment around us. Sadly, rubbish occasionally floats near the waterways, especially after busy weekends in the park. Once each month, club members assist with a river clean-up, collecting safe items from the banks while adults remove anything dangerous. Last summer, some members produced posters about litter. This year they have decided to create a short safety video showing new learners what to expect at their first session. It will be placed on the club website and shown at our welcome meeting. Parents will need to give permission for anyone appearing in it.||Students who attend all six beginner sessions are presented with a club badge at the end, and they may then apply for weekend activities. Our guest session this summer will be led by an athlete who specialises in kayak racing; she will talk about training and demonstrate some advanced techniques, although beginners will not be expected to copy them. Lastly, we need to replace several ageing paddles. The club will raise money through a sponsored paddle in August, with members completing a safe distance on the canal. Information about registration and medical forms is on the table by the door.
